title: Management of Hormone Replacement Therapy in Post-Reproductive-Aged Women

items.title: Why menopause is defined retrospectively after 12 months of amenorrhea
items.text: Menopause reflects permanent cessation of menses from follicular depletion; the diagnosis requires 12 consecutive months without menstruation by convention.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Menopause is defined as the permanent cessation of menses; by convention, the diagnosis of menopause is not made until the individual has had 12 months of amenorrhea."

items.title: How the menopausal transition presents hormonally
items.text: The transition is heralded by ovarian reserve depletion, cycle-length changes, reduced luteal progesterone, variable estradiol, and elevated FSH—often causing vasomotor symptoms before the final menstrual period.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The menopausal transition is heralded by ovarian follicular pool depletion (ovarian reserve), menstrual cycle length changes, reduced luteal phase progesterone synthesis, variable estradiol levels, and elevated FSH."

items.title: Why vasomotor symptoms occur in menopause
items.text: Hormonal fluctuations lower the hypothalamic threshold for heat dissipation (flushing) and raise the threshold for warming; KNDy neurons adjacent to the thermoregulatory center are hypothesized drivers.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "Thermoregulatory dysfunction begins in hypothalamus in response to hormonal fluctuations. The threshold in core temperature for cooling (flushes, sweats) is lowered"

items.title: How long vasomotor symptoms typically persist
items.text: Median duration is 7.4 years with mean post-FMP persistence of 4.5 years; duration varies by race, BMI, socioeconomic status, and depression.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "Vasomotor symptoms have a median duration of 7.4 years, with a mean post-final menstrual period persistence of 4.5 years."

items.title: Why genitourinary syndrome of menopause needs long-term therapy
items.text: Unlike vasomotor symptoms, genitourinary syndrome symptoms do not spontaneously resolve and may require ongoing treatment to preserve quality of life.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "Unlike vasomotor symptoms, symptoms of genitourinary syndrome of menopause do not spontaneously resolve over time. Therefore, long-term treatment may be required to preserve quality of life."

items.title: How effective hormone therapy is for menopausal symptoms
items.text: When used in the appropriate population, HT is highly effective for vasomotor symptoms, sleep disruption, mood, and genitourinary syndrome.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "When used in the appropriate patient population, hormone replacement therapy (HT) is highly effective and safe."

items.title: Why progestin is required with estrogen in women with a uterus
items.text: Chronic unopposed estrogen increases endometrial hyperplasia and cancer risk; progestin prevents estradiol-induced endometrial neoplasia.
items.reference: Uterine Neoplasia: "The incidence of uterine neoplasia is increased under conditions of chronic unopposed estrogen exposure in a woman with an intact uterus."

items.title: How route of administration affects thrombotic risk
items.text: Transdermal preparations may carry lower venous thromboembolism risk than oral estrogen while providing equivalent symptomatic efficacy.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Transdermal preparations may carry lower risk of thromboembolic events while providing the same efficacy for treatment management."

items.title: Why timing of HT initiation affects cognitive outcomes
items.text: HT in younger early-menopausal women does not impair cognition, whereas WHI Memory Study data suggest increased dementia when initiating HT after age 65.
items.reference: Cognitive Function: "the Women's Health Initiative Memory Study found an increased incidence of dementia in women older than 65 years initiating HT."

items.title: How to assess need when stopping menopausal HT
items.text: Stop HT 1 week before annual visit to assess symptom severity; most women taper slowly though 3%–15% have persistent severe symptoms requiring continued therapy or alternatives.
items.reference: Discontinuing Menopausal HT: "they should stop taking HT 1 week before an annual visit to allow assessment of symptom severity."

items.title: When laboratory tests help diagnose menopause
items.text: FSH, estradiol, or AMH may be indicated after hysterectomy without oophorectomy, age <40, estrogen-containing contraceptive use, or continued menses after age 55.
items.reference: diagnosis of Menopause: "Measurement of FSH and estradiol or antimullerian hormone may be indicated for women who: have had a hysterectomy without oophorectomy"

items.title: Why fear of hormones leads to undertreatment
items.text: WHI findings affected appropriate HT use; clinicians should offer evidence-based therapy and reevaluate periodically rather than defaulting to unproven compounded preparations.
items.reference: Summary: "fear of hormones has led to widespread undertreatment of symptoms and a proliferation of nonrigorously tested but popular treatments such as custom compounded hormones"

items.question: A 48-year-old African American woman has disruptive hot flashes. Which factor likely affects duration most among these?
items.explanation: African American women have longer duration and persistence of hot flashes compared with other racial/ethnic groups.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: A) Race"

items.question: A 48-year-old obese woman with PCOS has heavy bleeding after 6 months amenorrhea and wants contraception. Best treatment for bleeding?
items.explanation: Progestin IUD provides contraception and reduces endometrial neoplasia risk from ovulatory dysfunction; combined estrogen methods carry higher VTE/MI/breast cancer risk after age 45.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Progestin intrauterine device"

items.question: Menopause is diagnosed after:
items.explanation: By convention menopause requires 12 consecutive months without menses.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "the diagnosis of menopause is not made until the individual has had 12 months of amenorrhea."

items.question: Median age at natural menopause in the chapter is approximately:
items.explanation: Median age is 52.5 years though transition length varies widely.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Menopause typically occurs in the fifth to sixth decades of life, with a median age of 52.5 years."

items.question: Vasomotor symptoms affect up to what proportion of women?
items.explanation: Up to 80% experience vasomotor symptoms during the transition.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "Vasomotor symptoms affect up to 80% of women."

items.question: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause differs from vasomotor symptoms because:
options: It does not spontaneously resolve over time
items.explanation: GSM often needs long-term local or systemic therapy.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "symptoms of genitourinary syndrome of menopause do not spontaneously resolve over time."

items.question: During the menopausal transition, risk of new-onset major depression is increased:
items.explanation: Late transition is a vulnerability period for depression, linked to sleep disruption and night sweats.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "Women are 2- to 4-fold more likely to experience new-onset major depression during the menopausal transition than premenopausal women"

items.question: In a woman with an intact uterus, chronic unopposed estrogen increases risk of:
items.explanation: Progestin is indicated to oppose endometrial proliferation.
items.reference: Uterine Neoplasia: "The incidence of uterine neoplasia is increased under conditions of chronic unopposed estrogen exposure in a woman with an intact uterus."

items.question: Venous thromboembolism risk with HT is most related to:
items.explanation: Oral route and dose drive VTE risk; transdermal may be safer.
items.reference: Enous Thromboembolism: "The risk is related to oral administration and estradiol dosage."

items.question: WHI Memory Study suggested increased dementia when HT was initiated:
options: Within 1 year of final menstrual period only
items.explanation: Timing and age interact with cognitive outcomes of HT.
items.reference: Cognitive Function: "found an increased incidence of dementia in women older than 65 years initiating HT."

items.question: When planning to discontinue HT, symptom assessment is best done:
options: After stopping HT 1 week before the visit
items.explanation: Brief washout before annual visit clarifies ongoing symptom need.
items.reference: Discontinuing Menopausal HT: "they should stop taking HT 1 week before an annual visit to allow assessment of symptom severity."

items.question: FSH/estradiol testing for menopause diagnosis is indicated in:
options: A woman on estrogen-containing contraceptives with unclear status
Women with regular cycles under age 45 only
items.explanation: Labs help when history is unreliable—hysterectomy without oophorectomy, young age, OCP use, or bleeding after 55.
items.reference: diagnosis of Menopause: "are using estrogen-containing contraceptives (suppress FSH and elevate estradiol)"

items.question: Clinicians should prioritize:
options: Laser vaginal therapy as standard of care
items.explanation: Assess symptoms and offer evidence-based treatment with periodic reevaluation.
items.reference: Summary: "Clinicians should assess a patient's symptoms and offer evidence-based treatment, with periodic reevaluation of the necessity of treatment."

items.statement: Menopause reflects the loss of the finite oocyte pool produced during fetal development.
items.explanation: Reproductive senescence is a continuous process from menarche to depletion of follicles.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "It reflects the loss of the finite numbers of oocytes produced during fetal development."

items.statement: Hormone therapy is the most effective treatment for life-disrupting menopausal vasomotor symptoms.
items.explanation: HT remains the most effective option despite WHI concerns.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "HT is currently the most effective treatment for these life-disrupting symptoms."

items.statement: The average woman takes about 4 years to transition through menopause.
items.explanation: Wide individual variation exists around this median.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The average woman takes about 4 years to make the transition into menopause."

items.statement: KNDy neurons are hypothesized to drive vasomotor symptoms.
items.explanation: Kisspeptin/neurokinin B/dynorphin neurons sit adjacent to the thermoregulatory center.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "KNDy neurons (kisspeptin/neurokinin B/dynorphin), located adjacent to the thermoregulatory center, are hypothesized to be the primary driver of vasomotor symptoms."

items.statement: Laboratory tests are always required to diagnose menopause in all women.
items.explanation: History and exam suffice when other causes of amenorrhea are excluded.
items.reference: diagnosis of Menopause: "Laboratory tests are typically not indicated if history and physical examination rule out other potential causes of amenorrhea and bothersome symptoms."

items.statement: Transdermal estrogen may carry lower thromboembolic risk than oral estrogen.
items.explanation: Route modulates VTE risk while maintaining efficacy.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Transdermal preparations may carry lower risk of thromboembolic events while providing the same efficacy for treatment management."

items.statement: 3% to 15% of women have persistent severe vasomotor symptoms when attempting to stop HT.
items.explanation: A minority require continued hormones or nonhormonal alternatives.
items.reference: Discontinuing Menopausal HT: "a small proportion of women (3% to 15%) have persistence of severe symptoms and will require continued hormones or nonhormonal alternatives."

items.statement: Custom compounded hormones are rigorously tested replacements for FDA-approved HT.
items.explanation: Fear of hormones has driven proliferation of nonrigorously tested alternatives.
items.reference: Summary: "a proliferation of nonrigorously tested but popular treatments such as custom compounded hormones"

items.assertion: Assertion: Menopause requires 12 months of amenorrhea by convention.
items.reason: Reason: Hormonal changes never cause symptoms before cessation of menses.
items.explanation: Assertion true; symptoms often precede final menstrual period—reason false.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Menopause is defined as the permanent cessation of menses; by convention, the diagnosis of menopause is not made until the individual has had 12 months of amenorrhea."

items.assertion: Assertion: Women with a uterus need progestogen with systemic estrogen therapy.
items.reason: Reason: Progestogen is only for cosmetic skin benefits.
items.explanation: Assertion true for endometrial protection—reason false.
items.reference: Uterine Neoplasia: "the primary indication of progestin therapy during menopause is to prevent estradiol-induced endometrial hyperplasia and cancer."

items.assertion: Assertion: Vasomotor symptoms have a median duration of 7.4 years.
items.reason: Reason: All women resolve hot flashes within 1 year of the final menstrual period.
items.explanation: Assertion true; mean post-FMP persistence is 4.5 years—reason false.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "Vasomotor symptoms have a median duration of 7.4 years, with a mean post-final menstrual period persistence of 4.5 years."

items.assertion: Assertion: African American women tend to have longer hot flash duration.
items.reason: Reason: Race is the only modifier of vasomotor symptom duration.
items.explanation: Both race and other factors (BMI, SES, depression) modify duration—reason overstates.
items.reference: Case 1: "African American women are more likely to have a longer duration and persistence of hot flashes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause may require long-term treatment.
items.reason: Reason: GSM symptoms always resolve spontaneously like vasomotor symptoms.
items.explanation: Assertion true; GSM does not spontaneously resolve—reason false.
items.reference: Menopausal Symptoms: "symptoms of genitourinary syndrome of menopause do not spontaneously resolve over time."

items.assertion: Assertion: Initiating HT after age 65 may increase dementia risk.
items.reason: Reason: HT always improves cognition regardless of age.
items.explanation: Assertion supported by WHI Memory Study—reason false.
items.reference: Cognitive Function: "found an increased incidence of dementia in women older than 65 years initiating HT."

items.assertion: Assertion: VTE events are more likely in the first year of HT.
items.reason: Reason: VTE risk is unrelated to obesity or thrombophilia.
items.explanation: Assertion true; risk is magnified by cofactors—reason false.
items.reference: Enous Thromboembolism: "Venous thromboembolism events are more likely to occur within the first year of HT, and the risk is magnified with coexisting risk factors such as obesity or thrombophilia."

items.assertion: Assertion: HT within the first 5 years after final menstrual period may attenuate some hypogonadism-related morbidity.
items.reason: Reason: HT benefits and risks are identical for all organ systems regardless of timing.
items.explanation: Assertion from summary; risks/benefits vary by organ system and timing—reason false.
items.reference: Summary: "HT, when administered within the first 5 years of the final menopause attenuates some but not all adverse effects of menopause that are related to hypogonadism."
